content.problemStatement:
Nayra doesn't like stories of people receiving random arrays as birthday presents, but this time she received an array as a present for her own birthday! After struggling for a day trying to figure out what to do with this array, she asked Aryan for help. He gave her this problem.

You are given an array A=[A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \dots, A_N]A=[A1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč] containing NNN **distinct** integers. Count the number of ways to form (unordered) sets of disjoint increasing subsequences of AAA.

Formally, count the number of sets S={S1,S2,‚Ä¶,Sk}S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_k\}S={S1‚Äč,S2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,Sk‚Äč} such that:


Each SiS_iSi‚Äč is an increasing subsequence of AAA.
If i‚Č†ji \neq jiÓÄ†=j, SiS_iSi‚Äč and SjS_jSj‚Äč are disjoint, i.e, i‚Č†j‚ÄÖ‚Ää‚üĻ‚ÄÖ‚ÄäSi‚ą©Sj=‚ąÖi\neq j \implies S_i \cap S_j = \emptysetiÓÄ†=j‚üĻSi‚Äč‚ą©Sj‚Äč=‚ąÖ
Note that it is not necessary that the sequences S1,S2,‚Ä¶,SkS_1, S_2, \dots, S_kS1‚Äč,S2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,Sk‚Äč form a partition of AAA - in other words, some elements of AAA may not be in any chosen subsequence.

Two sets are considered equal if they contain the same subsequences. For example, the sets {[1,2],[3]}\{[1, 2], [3]\}{[1,2],[3]} and {[3],[1,2]}\{[3], [1, 2]\}{[3],[1,2]} are considered to be the same and should only be counted once.

Note that the final answer can be rather large, so compute its remainder after dividing it by 109+710^9 + 7109+7.

Please refer to the sample for a few examples.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the size of AAA.
The second line contains NNN distinct space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč - the elements of AAA.


Output Format
For each test, print one line containing a single integer, the answer to the problem modulo (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7).
constraints: The elements of AAA are distinct.
Each input file contains at most 333 tests with N>10N \gt 10N>10.
